3 Executive Summary The Laos RMG Impact Evaluation will provide rigorous evidence on the impacts of the Laos Poverty Reduction Fund’s Road Maintenance Groups program on the welfare and empowerment of women selected into the program. The Impact Evaluation uses a Randomized Control Trial (RCT) design, providing causal evidence on the effects of the program by analyzing outcomes before and after for women in the experimental sample, who were randomly assigned to either receive the program (“Treatment”) or not (“Control”). The results of the Impact Evaluation will be available after the endline data are collected after 18 months of program implementation. Baseline (i.e. pre-intervention) data were collected for the impact evaluation between September and October 2018. This data provides a glimpse of what program beneficiaries looked like before they started to receive salary payments under the RMG program. Under this study, baseline data collection was also designed to facilitate an analysis of the effectiveness of the targeting mechanism of the program. Village chiefs played a critical role in beneficiary selection. They informed women about the program and helped update poverty rankings on the spot, thus being the gatekeepers of eligibility. To check whether this process succeeded in identifying the poorest households, we surveyed a random sample of women in the village; comparing women in Control and Treatment groups who were deemed eligible for participation, to this random sample of women allows us to answer the question of whether village chiefs indeed selected the poorest households into the program. A number of interesting findings emerged from analysis of the baseline data,  There is a strong economic rationale for the intervention of offering jobs: The baseline data support the business case for the RMG intervention. This is for two reasons. First, the data show that opportunities for wage work are scarce in project areas. Most households contain several working- age adults but very few of these adults are employed in off-farm employment. Most appear to be employed on the household farm, even though average income for wage work (for the few who work) is much higher than the average earnings from the farm. However, such opportunities are even more scarce for women, indicating a strong potential need for a public works program with an explicit gender focus. Second, a large proportion of the study respondents indicated that their household had suffered from some type of economic shock in the prior two years. In addition, income for most households is highly concentrated, coming mainly from agriculture. Moreover, few households have access to savings or any type of financial services. Altogether, this paints a picture of a population that is highly vulnerable: shocks are common, and there are few insurance mechanisms to protect against these shocks. A public works program may help diversify household income and serve as one such insurance mechanism.  For a public works program that aims to create jobs, the program is solidly targeted: The PRF targets remote villages in rural Laos for infrastructure improvements, and through the RMG program, is offering opportunities for wage work for the poorest women within these villages. On both counts, the baseline data suggests that the RMG program is very well targeted.

4 The study sample (both Treatment and Control women) AND a randomly selected sample of women from RMG villages are worse off than a comparable sample of women drawn from a nationally representative survey. The women living in the RMG villages have fewer assets, live in houses with poorer characteristics and consume less food than rural women across Laos on average. Within these villages, the RMG program targets women from poor households. By all indications, the program seems to have been successful in this regard. Women that were interested and eligible for the RMG program (Treatment and Control women) were worse off on a number of characteristics than a sample of randomly selected women from the villages. The Treatment and Control women owned fewer assets, lived in houses with poorer characteristics and had lower incomes than a set of randomly selected women from the RMG villages. Not only do these women come from the poorest households, they are also less likely than other family members to be employed off-farm, reinforcing the message of solid targeting.  Participation of village heads in the selection strengthened instead of weakening the selection process: Village heads participated in the selection process and helped update a household’s poverty status from the 2016 PRF poverty ranking for households or household members that had either experienced an economic shock or had changed economic status for other reasons (for example through marriage). The data shows that the women identified as eligible for the program were poorer than other women in the village. Notably, the 2016 poverty rankings suggest the opposite relationship between these groups, which suggests that having village chiefs update poverty ranks on the day of the selection was important in identifying which households were poor in 2018. The report is structured as follows: we provide a description of the RMG program in Section 1 and selection into the RMG program in Section 2, including selection into Treatment and Control groups. Section 3 provides a description of the data collection process. Section 4 presents evidence on the comparability of Treatment and Control Groups. The remaining sections present results from baseline data analysis. Section 5 describes the study sample. Section 6 presents economic profiles of different groups in the study. Sections 7 describes empowerment indicators for women in the Treatment and Control groups. Section 8 provides evidence on how well the program’s targeting mechanisms worked. Section 9 includes information potentially relevant for program design: perceptions of fairness of the selection process and preferences for payments. Section 10 summarizes key findings from baseline data analysis and concludes.

5 1. Background 1.1. Road Maintenance Groups (RMGs) The Laos Poverty Reduction Fund (PRF), housed under the Government of Lao PDR Ministry of Agriculture and Forestry, was established in 2002 to help reduce poverty in rural Laos. PRF uses a community driven development approach that is centered around improvements to infrastructure in different sectors such as education, drinking water, irrigation, health and transport. PRF works in Kum Bans1 identified jointly by the government and PRF as being eligible for PRF funding, and Kum Bans in turn identify villages to receive support. PRF operates in 1,820 villages across 263 Kum Bans. While this approach has generally been successful in improving infrastructure, an evaluation of PRF’s projects carried out in 2015 highlighted that the post-completion sustainability of road projects was lacking as road quality deteriorated quickly due to a lack of maintenance. Therefore, PRF decided to introduce maintenance activities in its third organizational phase (PRF III, 2016-2019) with the aim of preventing improved roads from rapidly deteriorating and reducing beneficiaries’ access to services and markets. To maximize the developmental benefits of this activity, these jobs were offered to poor women living in these villages. These women typically have limited wage-earning opportunities and the intervention thus provided them with a basic income to supplement their livelihoods. By providing women, rather than men, with this income-earning opportunity, the project additionally aimed to further women’s empowerment and increase the likelihood of the additional income being used for household business, nutrition, and education investments. Each month PRF carries out road quality inspections to check the performance of the RMGs and applies deductions to their wage payments in the case of poor performance2. The payments are made based on the number of days worked, either monthly or quarterly according to the preference of the RMG members, using a fixed daily rate set at slightly below the prevailing market rate for labor in the village in order attract the poorest village members and preserve this job opportunity for the neediest. The number of days varies month-to month based on the maintenance needs but is typically a few days each month.3 A small pilot of this intervention was carried out in 2017 and qualitative data collected from some beneficiaries showed4 that the approach led to improved road conditions and access levels, while at the same time providing an important income opportunity for poor women in the villages. 1.2. RMG Impact Evaluation The World Bank’s East Asia and Pacific Gender Innovation Lab is partnering with PRF to carry out a rigorous impact evaluation - Randomized Control Trial (RCT) - of the RMG program to examine the 1 A Kum Ban is a group of villages. 2 The RMGs are paid for 50 person-days of work per km per year, divided into equal monthly payments. These are paid in full each month, unless the performance of the RMG is found to be poor, in which case a deduction is applied. The RMG members are free to decide how many days to work each month, as long as they complete the work. The project team monitors how many days they work each month to check if the assumed inputs and related payments are fair. 3 RMG members typically work more in the rainy season (e.g. 6-7 days per month) and less in the dry season (2-3). 4 However, no impact evaluation of the pilot has been carried out.

6 effects of participation in RMG jobs on women’s empowerment. The impact evaluation (IE) focuses on both short- and long-term effects of participation on these women. The evidence will be helpful from a knowledge as well as programmatic perspective. From a programmatic perspective, the IE will help PRF rigorously assess whether this program works in increasing women’s empowerment and in providing a social safety net for remote rural populations. The 2017 pilot suggested, anecdotally, improvements in incomes leading to an increase in consumption and investments in home improvements, education, healthcare and productive assets such as livestock, rice mills and motorcycles. The results of the IE will be able to rigorously demonstrate whether the RMG jobs lead to increased women’s empowerment and household welfare, including household incomes, consumption, and investments. The part-time nature of the maintenance work means that it will complement rather than replace other sources of income. The ability to manage other work concurrently with the RMG role will be investigated in the impact evaluation through inquiries into women’s labor force participation and time use. Additionally, the study will identify villagers’ and participants’ perspectives on the utility of the RMG program and the acceptability of the RMG members selection process. PRF will be able to use the study results in scaling up the program and making adjustments where desired. The study will also contribute to global knowledge in at least three ways. First, it will generate new evidence on what works to advance women’s empowerment. The maintenance contracts are set to run for 18 months (through December 2019), covering 2 full rainy seasons and providing approximately 75 days of employment for each RMG member (average of 4-5 days per month). The RMG members will be paid local market rates that tend to be around LAK 60,000/day (approx. US$7/day), or 200,000-250,000 LAK/month, resulting in a total average income of around $500 per RMG member over the implementation period. The pilot indicated that the RMG income makes up 25%-50% of annual household cash income. Impacts of women’s serving as significant household wage earners on their decision-making power, opinions regarding gender-based violence, and comfort with participating in community leadership activities will help elucidate functional methods for improving gender equity. Second, the IE will also provide important evidence on overall effectiveness of Public Works Programs (PWPs) as a poverty alleviation mechanism. To our knowledge, so far only two RCTs of PWPs have been carried out, both outside of Southeast Asia. These RCTs yield evidence contradictory to an extensive body of non-experimental (although rigorous) earlier studies. This work will be an important contribution to this debate. Finally, analysis of the efficacy of the selection process in recruiting poorer village members will also contribute to global research on optimal targeting strategies for social welfare programs. 2. RMG program selection The IE focuses on roads constructed during PRF Cycle 14. The road improvements under this cycle were completed in spring 2018. Selection of RMG members took place in June and early July 2018, with maintenance activities beginning in villages as soon as the village’s RMG members were selected. There are approximately 55 road segments covering 340 km of rural roads in 7 provinces (23-4 districts). To

7 carry out maintenance on these segments, 71 RMGs5 have been formed, providing part-time employment for 344 women. The jobs can be taken up by up to one woman from each household, aged 18-50, though neither upper nor lower age limits are strictly enforced. Preference is given to women from the poorest and/or female- headed households. These women are subsequently provided with basic hand tools and training to carry out routine road maintenance activities such as clearing roads of vegetation, clearing the drainage system and making small repairs to the road surface. As part of the impact evaluation, the selection of women from the eligible pool was randomized. Selection began with the village head of each participating village. The village head invited village members to the public meeting where the RMG jobs were to be advertised and RMG members selected. The village head was asked to invite all households with a woman aged over 18. At the meeting, PRF district staff and a data collection firm contracted under the World Bank, Indochina Research Laos, explained the job details, expected salary, and eligibility requirements to attendees. The project was presented as an effort to target poorer households. Attendees were also informed that selection would take place among eligible women via a lucky draw. Those who entered the lucky draw but did not win spots in the RMG would be entered onto a waitlist that would be drawn from in the event that any of the RMG women dropped out. The lucky draw staff explained that individuals could not trade spots between the RMG and waitlist groups, nor within the waitlist order. Interested women were then invited to register for the lucky draw. Each household was allowed to register one woman. PRF assigns households poverty ranks using a participatory approach. It had last conducted a poverty ranking exercise in 2016. This ranking assigned each household in PRF villages to 1 of 4 poverty levels: poorest, poor, middle-income, and better-off6. As each woman registered for the RMG lucky draw, lucky draw staff recorded the woman’s age and 2016 PRF poverty ranking. Seventeen women were also excluded on the basis of their poor health7. The village head advised on changes to household poverty status, which was necessary as since 2016, several households’ statuses had experienced economic shocks or changed due to marriages. The updated 2018 poverty ranking for each household was also recorded. Women were then entered into the lucky draw based on their 2018 poverty rankings. Each village had a target quota for RMG and waitlist members. The required number of RMG members depended on the length of road to be maintained—approximately 1 RMG member per kilometer. The waitlist quota was set at 1.5x the number of RMG members, rounding up to the nearest whole number. (For example, if a village required 5 RMG members, the waitlist target was 8, for a total target of 13 women to enter the lucky draw.) Women were entered by poverty ranking bracket. All poorest and poor households were automatically eligible to enter the lucky draw. If too few poorest and poor women registered for the lucky draw, interested and eligible women in the middle-income ranking were entered. If there were still 5 Because of an error in the randomized RMG selection process, only 70 of the RMGS and 339 of the RMG members are included in the impact evaluation. Narm tar lang Village in Viengphoukha District, Luangnamtha Province is participating in the RMG project but is not included in the impact evaluation sample. 6 These rankings combined proxy assessments of income and wealth with a community validation process. 7 The age ranges of these women were: 1 in her 70’s, 4 in their 60’s, 11 in their 50’s, and 1 in her 40’s.

8 too few women, those ranked better-off were also entered. Poverty ranking brackets were included as whole brackets—no divisions were made between women in the same bracket. Once the number of interested and eligible women had been tallied, staff announced the names of those who were ultimately eligible to enter the lucky draw. The lucky draw system was explained: each woman would draw a numbered ping pong ball from an opaque box. Those with the lowest numbers would enter the RMG and the remaining women would enter the waitlist in the order of the numbers they drew. For example, if a village required 5 RMG members and 20 women entered the lucky draw, those who drew numbers 1-5 entered the RMG, the woman who drew #6 was in 1st place on the waitlist, the woman who drew #7 was in 2nd place on the waitlist, etc. The lucky draw was then conducted in front of the assembly. As each woman selected a ping pong ball, her drawn ball was displayed to the crowd, announced aloud, and recorded. There were no complaints regarding the procedure or results. 3. Baseline data 3.1. Data collection Baseline data collection took place after the RMGs were formed but before any salary payments were made to RMG members8. Collecting data before RMG members received any salary payments allows the impact evaluation to capture the “status quo” in terms of economic conditions and empowerment. When endline data is collected, after approximately 18 months of salary payments, the study will be able to compare between the two states, producing a rigorous examination of the effects of these payments on the welfare of the households and women’s empowerment covering their decision-making power, roles in community leadership, and related matters. Data was collected by Indochina Research Laos (IRL) under supervision of the World Bank (WB). Data collection began in September 2018 and concluded within the first week of October 2018. Nine (9) teams of 5 enumerators and 1 team leader (54 individuals total) surveyed RMG and waitlist (WL) women, as well as a random sample (RS) of women from each RMG village. These teams additionally surveyed the village heads on RMG selection preferences. A separate set of 7 enumerators collected data on the prices of common products in the primary local markets in each village in RMG villages and their neighboring villages. The RMG/WL/RS, village head, and market price surveys are described further in Section 3.2. The survey methodology and instruments were approved by an international ethical review board: HML Institutional Review Board, based in Washington, DC. The instruments were tested and refined through two rounds of piloting conducted prior to interviews with any of the study participants. These pilots were conducted in Sangthong District, Vientiane, chosen balancing the needs of convenience with testing on a population similar to the study sample (i.e. living in rural or semi-rural settings). Over 30 pilot interviews were conducted. All participants were informed of the study objectives, partners, and general survey content and expected interview length when solicited for interviews. Each participant’s informed consent was 8 Salary payments were rolled out as project areas completed baseline data collection.

9 required before beginning any interview. Confidentiality was ensured for each participant. After concluding the interview, each participant was presented with a printed family photograph as a thank- you gift for their participation in the study. IRL implemented quality control measures by: using electronic tablets for data collection to minimize data entry errors, observing enumerator interviews in the field, reviewing data as it was collected for errors and discrepancies, and checking final data as it was cleaned for gaps and errors. Data collection was carried out using tablets for most of the surveys. In fifty-five (55) cases, surveys were carried out using paper questionnaires because of technical issues with the tablets; these were later entered digitally. The tablets were designed with skip-patterns built-in (i.e. to automatically skip irrelevant questions), based on respondents’ prior answers. The tablets also recorded segments of the interviews9. Team leaders sat in on a random selection of enumerators every day to oversee the survey procedures. 10% of each enumerator’s interviews were evaluated in this way. Senior IRL survey staff (Survey Director, Fieldwork Manager, and Fieldwork Supervisor) additionally randomly observed interviews for quality. IRL staff based in Vientiane reviewed audio files of recorded interview segments for errors and conducted back-checks to verify the validity of the recorded answers10. Discrepancies identified via back- checks were investigated and resolved. The collected data as a whole was assessed for outliers and gaps. WB staff also oversaw enumerator training and selection, observed data collection in the field, and reviewed final data for quality. 3.2. Survey instruments Several questionnaires were administered under the baseline survey: Household Questionnaire (HQ), Individual Questionnaire (IQ), Random Sample Questionnaire (RSQ), Targeting Survey, and Market Price Survey.  HQ: Administered to RMG and WL households. Any member of the household knowledgeable about household income, labor, assets, and related issues was eligible to answer the questions.  IQ: Administered to the members of the RMG and WL groups. These participants could not be replaced if the RMG or WL woman was not available or refused to be interviewed.  RSQ: Administered to a random sample of households in the RMG villages who had at least one female member aged 18-55. The questionnaire was identical to the Household Questionnaire, with the exception of including 2 additional modules: Poverty Rankings and Connections. A female HH member aged 18-55 was randomly sampled within each RS household to answer the Nutrition module. RS households and women could be replaced if the original selections were unavailable or refused to participate. 9 Complete audio recordings were unfeasible given the 2-hour length of the interviews and associated large audio files. These were too large to transmit to the central, Vientiane-based IRL office from the survey locations in remote villages. 55 question were chosen for recording based on complexity of the questions, with the more complex questions being recorded. The recording of these questions was unknown to the data collection team. 10 Central IRL staff reviewed 25 of the recorded 55 questions for each enumerator daily and, without referencing the enumerator’s answer, recorded their own answers to the questions based on the recordings. Answers were then compared between those recorded by central IRL and those by the enumerators.

10  Targeting Survey: Administered to the village heads. It asked them to rank 15 women and their households (5 RMG, 5 WL, and 5 RS) according to wealth proxies and suitability for being an RMG member.  Market Price Survey: Recorded the prices of common items sold in local markets. It was conducted in 85 RMG villages and 73 villages neighboring the RMG villages but without RMGs of their own. Table 1: Modules within Household, Individual, and Random Sample Questionnaires. Sample RMG and WL households The target sample for data collection was based on the number of women recruited into RMGs via lucky draw (339) and those who entered the waitlist (843), as well as 1 targeting survey and 1 market price survey per RMG village (87 of each survey), and 734 Random Sample households and women. Some RMG and WL households and/or women were temporarily unavailable, refused to participate in the interviews, or had moved out of the villages and could not be interviewed. Also, several villages

12 merged. Therefore, completed interviews were: 333 RMG women, 813 WL women, 742 RS households/women, 85 village heads (targeting survey) and 158 market price surveys. Random Sample Women To analyze differences between the households in our study and other households in their villages, a subset of the data was also collected from randomly selected households in RMG villages. For some villages, PRF had relatively complete list of households in the village from the PRF 2016 poverty ranking11. In these villages, “Random Sample” households were selected in advance12. For villages where PRF did not have an up-to-date list of households, IRL first met with the village head to list all households in the village with at least 1 female member aged 18-55. Then the households were randomly selected13. IRL used Kish grids to select the woman to interview within each Random Sample Household (only women answered the Nutrition module). Market Price Survey Eighty-five (85) villages are covered by the 70 RMGs included in the impact evaluation study. Market price surveys were conducted in these 85 villages and 73 neighboring villages at baseline. IRL gathered information about where the majority of the people in the community did their grocery shopping for different types of products and collected price data for these products from those markets. For example, if most villagers bought everyday food items in village shops and say, food seasoning in a larger non- local market, the price survey of everyday food items would be collected from the village shops while the prices of food seasoning would be taken from the larger market. 4. Did randomization work? As described previously, women were selected for RMGs at random from among a pool of eligible women. Women who were not selected for the RMG were assigned to the waitlist. If the randomization was successful, these two groups should be roughly similar since the only difference between them is that every woman in one group randomly selected a ping-pong ball of a lower number. Because the impact evaluation compares the differences before and after receiving wage payments between RMG and WL women, it is important to know whether there were any pre-existing differences between the two groups at baseline (i.e. even before the program was active). 4.1. Basic demographics The tables below compare several characteristics of RMG and WL women. In general, the tables show the average value for each of the two groups, the difference between them and the ‘p-value’ of that difference. The p-value is the probability of observing a difference of that size in a random subset of the population, even if the means of the two populations were equal. A p-value under 0.10 indicates that the probability of randomly seeing this difference if the two groups had the same average would be less 11 A household list was considered sufficiently complete if more than 55% of the households could be identified through the 2016 poverty rankings sheet during the RMG member selection process. The Random Sample was selected manually in villages with outdated lists or where the poverty lists did not note the households’ ethnicity. 12 WB also provided a replacement (replaced if without any female members aged 18-55/after 3 attempts to reschedule/refused to participate/if already an RMG or WL household). 13 IRL used a random number table to select the households in the field. For villages where we had pre-existing listing data, randomization was carried out on Stata.

13 than 10%, i.e. not very likely. We use the p-value as a simple way of checking whether the two groups in fact are similar (share the same means). The tables below suggest that randomization was effective in creating two roughly similar groups. There are very few characteristics14 in which the differences between the groups are statistically significant at baseline. Their households primarily occupy 1 building with 1 or 2 rooms. Few have improved, brick walls or concrete roofs. Slightly over half lack toilets, and less than half have electricity. Almost all cook with firewood and about half cook inside their homes. However, most obtain their drinking water from a piped source or a well. Housing is also a proxy for the wealth of the household, so this is also partially suggestive evidence for wealth differences between the two groups being small. Under assets, we include a broad range of items covering productive assets like land and livestock and durable consumer goods. The full list is too long to present here, but Table 5 shows both RMG and WL households own similar quantities of durable assets for a selected subset of items, which were included into the questionnaires. Both RMG and WL households possess few durable assets, particularly productive assets. These are not highly common, not even transport assets (which road maintenance could increase the value of). 4.3. Economic shocks This feeds into one of the strongest findings that emerges from the baseline data—that economic shocks are highly common in the study sample. Over 90% of study households reported having experienced at least one type of economic shock in the prior two years. The most common shocks are the loss of livestock, illness or injury of a family member and crop loss. The death of a family member and drought are also relatively common. These types of shocks can have serious consequences for well- off households but in a population that is already poor, the consequences can be dire. The prevalence of shocks makes vulnerability an important consideration from a macro perspective: even households that are able to escape poverty in one year remain highly susceptible to slipping back into poverty. Subjective well-being The emerging picture is one of the study sample being located in remote rural Laos, lacking in access to economic opportunities. In order to capture other dimensions of welfare, we also asked the households directly about their own assessment of their well-being. These responses paint a happier picture. Only a small proportion of the respondents report dissatisfaction with the quality of their life and well over 3/4ths of the population report being satisfied. On a second question that asked them where they would place themselves on a six-step ladder going from poorest to richest, a large proportion of the population placed themselves on the bottom two rungs of the ladder. Given that these are largely households that are in the bottom two ranks of the PRF poverty rankings, this is not a huge surprise. What is interesting here is the responses about where this population saw itself 5 years prior and 5 years ahead suggests that they are optimistic. However, some of these are psychometric measurements requiring more careful validation and analysis, which we are still in the process of carrying out. We plan to put out a separate note on this topic. However, for the purpose of this report, we report women’s responses on two measures – participation in decision making in their households and participation in the community. 7.1. Targeting One of the principal objectives behind the baseline survey in the Laos RMG IE was to analyze how well the RMG program was targeted. Within each village, PRF aimed to target the RMG program to women in the poorest households. In practice, women from these households were given preference in the selection process over women from more well-off households. However, the process relied on inputs from the village heads – the village heads were responsible for letting villagers know in advance on which day to show up for the selection and for updating the poverty rank of the household on the day of the selection if it was deemed necessary. In both steps, there is potential for bias, arising either from a lack of information on part of the village head on who the poorest households in the village are, or potentially from a deliberate attempt to direct these jobs towards members of their choosing, who may or may not be poor. In order to test this, we collected baseline data from randomly selected households within the village with women of eligible age (we refer to this sample as the “Random Sample” or “Other women”). Comparing the characteristics of these households to the women and households in our study sample should tell us whether our study sample is indeed poorer than other households in the village, as the PRF intended. In short, the data suggests that this was the case. First, comparing the women in the study sample to the other women in the village on basic demographic characteristics, we find that the women in the study sample are less likely to be married than other

29 women in the village. All other differences are not statistically significant. Notably, the difference in the likelihood of being married is higher. This may well be because female-headed households are households of women who are either widowed or separated and therefore missing at least one income earner, and who may have therefore been prioritized in the poverty rankings to receive the lower ranks. As such, we view it is a positive sign for the pro-poor targeting of the program. 8.1. Perceptions regarding RMG program The baseline data was explicitly designed to include a Targeting Survey20. This survey enables us to explore whether relying on village head recommendations is an effective way to identify and offer program benefits to the poorest community members. However, the Targeting Survey does not address whether relying on village heads is acceptable to community members. To address this question, we asked RMG and WL their opinions on the fairness and validity of the selection process in the Individual Questionnaire. The questionnaire also inquired into women’s opinions on the RMG program itself, such as whether the RMGs could be effective in maintaining road quality. During pilot program implementation and organization of the baseline survey, PRF and the IE team had discussed whether RMG members might have a strong preference for either monthly or quarterly payments. The survey asked about this, as well. Results on payment preferences can inform future RMG organization, as well as other workfare programs PRF or others may lead in the future. 9.1. 32 Overall, more than 2/3 of the IE sample thought it was “absolutely” fair for the village head to advise the selection team on the poverty status of the applicants. During the selection process, the selection team clearly informed the community members that poverty status was central to selection into the RMGs. By approving the village head’s role in clarifying poverty status, the women are supporting the village head’s power in allocating social benefits. 33 RMG members have a slight preference for being paid every 3 months over being paid monthly. They feel it is more exciting to receive larger amounts of cash and that they will be able to buy something larger with the larger lump sum. Capacity building in financial planning and saving would likely be useful for these individuals. Those who prefer monthly payments mainly like the ability to pay small bills quickly. They also like the sense of stability in the incoming cash flow. Several individuals felt enabled by smaller payments to buy items that their husbands would reject. 34 RMG and WL women were also asked if they thought the RMGs would be able to maintain the road quality. Over half of both assignment groups strongly believe the RMGs will be effectives. Just over 25% think the RMG will likely be able to maintain the road. RMG members were more likely to “absolutely” believe in their ability to maintain the road. For all other levels of confidence, WL members expressed a little more doubt. This result is not unusual, given that WL members were reporting on the efficacy of a job they were denied. 10. Conclusion and next steps The results of the baseline survey indicate success along two streams: one, the IE randomization appears to have been successful and free of bias, which is important to ensure that the study will generate rigorous evidence downstream after endline data are collection. Two, the data from the baseline show that the pro-poor targeting successfully identified poorer villages and households to receive social welfare benefits. Similarities between the RMG and WL members across a range of characteristics shows the two groups to be nearly identical. RMG and WL women resemble each other in age, education, ethnicity, assets, housing conditions, and nutrition intake. This suggests that the randomization was successful. The randomization of the experimental sample into the RMG and Waitlist groups is an important element of the design of the study and knowing this process was free of bias allows us to be more confident that the effects of participation, assessed based on the endline data, will be rigorous evidence of the program’s effects. Second, the comparison of the study sample with other households in the village shows that the program was successfully able to target poorer households. Households in the study sample are poorer than randomly selected households in RMG villages on a number of different indicators of wealth. Finally, both the study sample and other women in their village are significantly poorer than a sample of rural women in the same age group from a nationally representative survey. Women in the RMG villages have lower income and education levels, poorer housing conditions, and consume less food. This shows that the villages PRF works in are poorer than the national average and PRF thus successfully targets areas to receive social welfare benefits. This and other findings, such as the scarcity of wage-earning jobs in program areas and the frequency of economic shocks are suggestive that the RMG program can have a meaningful impact on the beneficiaries. The endline survey will be conducted in October and November 2019. Data collected during this time will capture the effects of approximately one-and-a-half years of increased income from RMG salaries. The IE will look for changes from baseline data in earned income, wealth, food consumption, time use, and indicators of women’s empowerment. An increase in household income among RMG but not WL households, or an increase in hours of school attended for RMG household children but not WL household children, for example, would indicate positive impacts from the RMG program in improving household wealth. An increase among RMG members in participating in household decisions or community leadership activities, for example, would indicate positive program impacts on women’s empowerment.

35 Findings from the IE will be shared with PRF to support program development. Results will also be shared with the broader global community to support knowledge development and program design in workfare and female empowerment programs worldwide. 11. 37 Household income and wealth The patterns of household income suggest that Lao-Tai are more well off, but with Mon-Khmer and Hmong households specializing in Agriculture and Livestock respectively. The total household income of Lao-Tai is significantly higher than other ethnic groups. Income among Chinese-Tibetan women in our sample is less than half the income of Lao-Tai women. Lao-Tai households seem to earn more particularly in Handicraft production, consistent with what we see in the time-use patterns. However, the other ethnic groups seem to earn less than Lao-Tai across the board – the two noteworthy exceptions are Agriculture and Livestock, where Mon-Khmer and Hmong households respectively earn more. In the case of Livestock, Hmong households earn more than twice as much as other households. Other ethnic groups also lag behind the Lao-Tai in earning from wage work, though the differences are not statistically significant. 39 Likelihood of being in RMG group One last dimension on which we compare the different ethnic groups is on the likelihood of being a part of our study sample (either treatment or control). This is a test of whether randomization worked, and whether there was a bias in the selection process pre-randomization favoring one group over another. If randomization was carried out correctly, we should not expect there to be any differences between the different ethnicities in terms of the likelihood of being in the treatment or control groups. However, the step preceding randomization – where women were informed about the RMG jobs and then declared eligible or not based on their poverty rank on the day of selection – could be biased if the village head, for instance, favored one groups over another. Comparing the likelihood of the different ethnic groups being either in the control group, or in our study sample reveals that there were no statistically significant differences between the groups in either of these two regards.
